chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: The toxin is only released if the hard seed coat of the Rosary Pea is chewed, crushed, or otherwise broken1. Because the seed coat is so hard, if a cat swallows the seed whole and it remains intact, it is generally considered non-toxic1. If the seed is chewed and the toxin is released, severe cases require veterinary treatment for electrolyte replacement
chemicals.description: in cats: Abrin is a highly dangerous toxin that works by inhibiting cellular protein synthesis1. When absorbed, it leads to severe gastrointestinal signs, which can cause significant dehydration and dangerous electrolyte imbalances
chemicals.name: Abrin (a plant lectin or toxalbumin related to ricin)

description: Rosary Pea (Abrus precatorius), also known as Jequirity Bean, is one of the most acutely toxic plants on earth. Its seeds contain abrin — a ribosome-inactivating protein (RIP) closely related to ricin — that is lethal in nanogram quantities. The hard seed coat usually prevents toxicity if seeds are swallowed whole, but chewed or cracked seeds are life-threatening.
emergencyNote: Assessment of Dehydration and Electrolyte Status
name: Rosary Pea
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: abrin (ribosome-inactivating protein) in seeds; leaves contain abrine alkaloid
Mechanism: abrin halts protein synthesis in cells by inactivating ribosomes; affected cells die; organs fail progressively
Symptoms (delayed 2–6h): vomiting, diarrhea, weakness, abdominal pain; severe cases cause multi-organ failure, seizures, and death
Intact swallowed seeds may pass without harm; cracked or chewed seeds are a medical emergency
Any suspected abrin exposure is a veterinary emergency — do not wait for symptoms

symptoms.name: Gastrointestinal signs (which typically lead to significant dehydration and electrolyte imbalances)
symptoms.notes: Abrin is a plant lectin (toxalbumin) that works by inhibiting cellular protein synthesis1. When absorbed, the resulting gastrointestinal distress can cause severe dehydration and dangerous electrolyte imbalances1. In these severe cases, the cat will require veterinary treatment for fluid and electro

treatments.description: The veterinarian will examine the cat to evaluate the severity of the gastrointestinal signs and determine the extent of any dehydration and electrolyte imbalances
treatments.name: Assessment of Dehydration and Electrolyte Status
treatments.notes: This initial assessment is critical because if the cat swallowed the seeds whole without chewing them, it is usually non-toxic and may not require further medical intervention1. 2. Systemic Supportive Care

treatments.description: The veterinary team will administer fluids and electrolytes to restore the cat's normal physiological balance and counteract the fluid loss caused by the gastrointestinal symptoms
treatments.name: Electrolyte Replacement Treatment
treatments.notes: This therapy is specifically indicated and necessary for severe cases where the seed coat was broken, releasing the toxin (abrin) and causing significant illness
